summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she has completed the first draft of her book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' with a May 2016 publication date. She also shares that she'll be starting part-time work at the GovLab on data questions for the Department of Justice. The post reflects on the different mindsets required for book writing versus blogging, expressing enthusiasm about returning to short-form writing. She notes that being annoyed helps fuel good blog posts, and expresses gratitude for the experts who generously shared their time during her book research.
key_points: First draft of 'Weapons of Math Destruction' is complete, with May 2016 publication planned
New part-time position at GovLab working on Department of Justice data questions
Book writing requires carrying enormous context; blogging requires one idea expressed well
Being annoyed is good fuel for blog posts
People are extremely generous with their time and expertise when you're researching a book
money_quotes: When you write a book you have to carry around in your head an enormous amount of context, but when you write a blogpost you just need to have one idea and to say it well.
It also helps if you're annoyed.
I'm pretty good at being annoyed, and I love and miss being mathbabe.
Turns out people are extremely generous with their time and expertise, and I am grateful for that!
key_insight: Book writing and blogging require fundamentally different mental approaches—books demand sustained context while blogs thrive on singular, well-expressed ideas fueled by productive annoyance.
